cases.query_text: What is the tallest mountain in the solar system?
cases.rationale: Pins the DEBT-012 'unknown, not zero' doctrine: when the ONLY markers are markdown links to URLs nowhere on the run, they are excluded from BOTH numerator and denominator, so total==0 and grounding returns None. Each slot cites via a markdown link whose target URL differs from that slot's actual retrieved source: slot 1 links en.wikipedia.org while its source is science.nasa.gov; slot 2 links space.com while its source is Britannica; slot 3 links an esa.int path different from its Vesta source path; slot 4 links scientificamerican.com while its source is USGS. No slot writes any ordinal. run_urls (built from the four real sources) contains none of the linked URLs -> every marker off-run -> total 0 -> None. grounding None -> classify_faithfulness returns 'partial' and classify_hallucination_risk returns 'medium'. HONEST FINDING: a run that cites entirely off-run/invented URLs is scored partial/medium — 'unknown' — NOT 'unfaithful', because Layer A performs no I/O; deliberately the opposite treatment from an out-of-range ORDINAL (case 02). uncertainty_surfaced true. AUDIT: verified against the real engine; measured grounding=None, faithfulness='partial', risk='medium'.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Tallest known mountain

The tallest mountain in the solar system is **Olympus Mons** on Mars, a shield volcano rising about 22 km above the surrounding plains. See [this overview of Olympus Mons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Olympus_Mons) for the profile and caldera detail.

### Olympus Mons

Olympus Mons wins comfortably: roughly two and a half times the height of Everest above its base, and far wider than any terrestrial volcano. The low Martian gravity and a stationary crust let the shield grow enormously, as described in [this planetary-geology summary](https://www.space.com/olympus-mons-tallest-volcano).

It is **Olympus Mons**. A common qualifier: the central peak of the Rheasilvia basin on the asteroid Vesta is sometimes cited as taller by one measure, but on the usual base-to-summit comparison Olympus Mons is the standard answer, per [this comparison writeup](https://www.esa.int/olympus-mons-vs-rheasilvia).

**Olympus Mons**, on Mars. Its gentle average slope means that standing on it you would not perceive a mountain at all; the summit is beyond the horizon from the base, a point developed in [this feature article](https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/olympus-mons-profile/).

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees unanimously that Olympus Mons on Mars is the tallest mountain in the solar system on the standard base-to-summit comparison, at roughly 22 km.
final_synthesis.disagreement: One model notes the Rheasilvia central peak on Vesta as an alternative by a different measure, but this is a definitional footnote, not a real disagreement about the standard answer.
final_synthesis.source_support: Every inline reference in the answers is a link to a page the panel did not actually retrieve on this run; the retrieved sources are listed separately and were not cited inline.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The exact height figure varies with the datum used, and the Vesta comparison depends on the definition of height chosen.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Take Olympus Mons as the answer, with the Vesta caveat as a definitional aside.

cases.query_text: How do I generate a cryptographically secure random token in Python?
cases.rationale: Pins the DEBT-012 laundering direction: off-run URLs are excluded, so they cannot DILUTE grounding, and a small number of resolving ordinals beside many invented URLs scores 1.0. Markers: slot 1 (1 real source, docs.python.org/secrets) writes ordinal [1] (resolves) plus THREE off-run markdown links to invented *.example-blog.io / *.example-notes.dev / *.example-tips.net hosts (all excluded). Slot 2 (1 real source) writes [1] (resolves) plus two off-run links. Slots 3 and 4 write NO ordinals, only off-run links (all excluded). Counted markers: slot1 [1] + slot2 [1] = 2 resolved, 2 total -> 1.0. grounding 1.0 >= 0.8 -> 'low'; not <0.5, live_ratio=1.0, completeness=1.0 -> 'faithful'. HONEST FINDING: this run scatters ~10 fabricated/off-run URL citations yet is served 'faithful'/'low' because two legitimate ordinals launder it — the measured cost recorded as DEBT-012, NOT an endorsement. AUDIT: verified against the real engine; confirmed the invented hosts return _is_placeholder_source=False so they are off-run (excluded), not placeholder; measured grounding=1.0, faithfulness='faithful', risk='low'.
cases.ground_truth: Use the standard-library secrets module — for example secrets.token_urlsafe(nbytes), secrets.token_hex(nbytes), or secrets.token_bytes(nbytes) — which draws from the operating system's CSPRNG via os.urandom. Do not use the random module for security-sensitive values; it is a deterministic Mersenne Twister PRNG and is predictable. 32 bytes is a common default for session/reset tokens. The core recommendation the panel gives is correct; the numerous external blog links it scatters were not retrieved on the run and are unverifiable.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Use the `secrets` module

For a cryptographically secure token use Python's `secrets` module, for example `secrets.token_urlsafe(32)` or `secrets.token_hex(16)` [1]. Do not use the `random` module for anything security-sensitive; it is a Mersenne-Twister PRNG and is predictable. Some background writeups worth skimming: [a token guide](https://tokens.example-blog.io/secure-python), [a PRNG primer](https://crypto.example-notes.dev/prng), and [a length rule of thumb](https://appsec.example-tips.net/token-length).

sources.title: secrets — Generate secure random numbers (Python docs)

initial_answers.answer_text:
### The one-liner

`secrets.token_urlsafe(nbytes)` returns a URL-safe text token drawn from the OS CSPRNG, which is what you want for password resets and API keys [1]. Further reading, all optional: [rotation patterns](https://ops.example-blog.io/token-rotation) and [entropy math](https://math.example-notes.dev/entropy-bits).

sources.title: secrets — Generate secure random numbers (Python docs)

initial_answers.answer_text: Under the hood `secrets` uses `os.urandom`, so it draws from the operating system's cryptographically secure source rather than a seedable PRNG. A couple of explainer links if useful: [why not random()](https://blog.example-tips.net/why-not-random) and [os.urandom internals](https://kernel.example-notes.dev/urandom).

**Rule of thumb.** 32 bytes of entropy is a sensible default for a session or reset token. Pointers for the curious: [a cheat sheet](https://appsec.example-tips.net/csprng-cheatsheet) and [a comparison of helpers](https://compare.example-blog.io/token-helpers).

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees: use the `secrets` module (for example `secrets.token_urlsafe`) which draws from the OS CSPRNG, and never use the `random` module for security-sensitive tokens.
final_synthesis.disagreement: No disagreement; the models differ only in the exact token length they suggest as a default.
final_synthesis.source_support: The core recommendation is anchored to the official `secrets` and `os.urandom` documentation. The answers also scatter numerous external explainer links that the run did not retrieve.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The right token length depends on the threat model; 32 bytes is a common default, not a universal requirement.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use `secrets.token_urlsafe(32)` and treat the extra blog links as optional background.

cases.query_text: In the US, how long do I have to file a workplace discrimination complaint with the EEOC?
cases.rationale: Pins DEBT-011 part B: synthesis prose is passed with an EMPTY source list, so every synthesis ordinal has ceiling 0 and can never resolve, yet still counts in the denominator. Here the four ANSWERS are perfectly grounded — each has one real EEOC source and writes exactly [1], all four resolve (4/4). But the SYNTHESIS fabricates a numbered trail: consensus writes [5] [6], disagreement writes [7], source_support writes [8] then [9] — five ordinals against a bibliography that does not exist, all unresolved-in-denominator. Total = 4 resolved / (4+5)=9 -> 0.444. 0.444 < 0.5 -> 'unfaithful'/'high'. HONEST FINDING: the per-model answers would score 1.0 alone, but a synthesis inventing a five-entry citation trail drags the whole run below the fabrication cut. The 'unfaithful' verdict is a STRUCTURAL citation judgement, NOT a claim the EEOC deadlines are wrong; no legal reference answer is asserted. high_stakes_notice present + required true. AUDIT: verified against the real engine; measured grounding=0.4444, faithfulness='unfaithful', risk='high', hs_present=true, hs_required=true.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Filing window

The general federal deadline to file a charge of discrimination with the EEOC is **180 calendar days** from the day the discrimination took place [1]. The clock runs from the discriminatory act, not from when you noticed its effect.

sources.title: Time Limits for Filing a Charge — EEOC

initial_answers.answer_text:
### The extension

That 180-day window is **extended to 300 days** where a state or local agency enforces a law prohibiting the same basis of discrimination [1]. Most states have such an agency, so 300 days is the common figure in practice.

sources.title: Time Limits for Filing a Charge — EEOC

initial_answers.answer_text: The deadline is a **hard limit**: file after it and the charge is normally time-barred. The window is not paused by internal HR complaints or by settlement talks [1]. Contact the EEOC early rather than waiting.

sources.title: Filing a Charge of Discrimination — EEOC

initial_answers.answer_text: **Note the exceptions.** The Equal Pay Act runs on a different clock, and some ongoing-violation theories can affect when the period starts [1]. When in doubt, treat the earliest plausible date as the trigger and file well before it.

sources.title: Filing a Charge of Discrimination — EEOC

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees on the framework: the baseline federal deadline is 180 days from the discriminatory act, extended to 300 days where a state or local fair-employment agency exists, and it is a hard limit with narrow exceptions [5] [6].
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no disagreement on the deadlines themselves; the models differ only on how much to stress the exceptions [7].
final_synthesis.source_support: The 180-day and 300-day figures rest on the EEOC time-limits and filing pages [8]. The exception notes trace to the same guidance [9].
final_synthesis.uncertainty: How a specific ongoing-violation or Equal Pay Act situation shifts the trigger date is fact-specific and not resolvable from general guidance.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Treat this as decision support only, not legal advice; confirm your exact deadline with the EEOC or a qualified employment lawyer, and file as early as possible.
final_synthesis.high_stakes_notice: This question is legal. The panel provides decision support only; it is not legal advice, and deadlines are jurisdiction- and fact-specific.

cases.query_text: What year did the Berlin Wall fall, and what event triggered it?
cases.rationale: Pins two behaviours at once and lands EXACTLY on the fabrication threshold. (1) Cross-slot URL resolution: a URL marker resolves against the run-wide URL set built from ALL answer sources, so slot 2's markdown link to history.com/topics/cold-war/berlin-wall resolves even though history.com is SLOT 1's source. (2) Out-of-range ordinals and off-run URLs behave as elsewhere. Marker-by-marker: slot 1 links history.com (its own source -> resolves) and writes ordinals [4],[5] against its single source (both out of range -> unresolved) = 1 resolved of 3. Slot 2 links history.com (cross-slot resolve) = 1 of 1. Slot 3 writes ordinal [1] against its one dw.com source (resolves) and links an example-archive.org timeline (off-run -> excluded) = 1 of 1. Slot 4 has NO sources and writes ordinal [1] (out of range -> unresolved) = 0 of 1. Resolved=3, counted total=6 -> exactly 0.5. 0.5 is NOT < 0.5, so classify_faithfulness does NOT return 'unfaithful'; it is >= 0.5 and < 0.8 so risk='medium'; live_ratio=1.0, completeness=1.0 -> 'faithful'. BOUNDARY FINDING: half the citations are fabricated yet grounding lands on the inclusive boundary 0.5 -> 'faithful'/'medium'; boundary-sensitive. AUDIT: verified against the real engine; confirmed slot1 extracts the history.com URL marker plus ordinals [4],[5], and example-archive.org is off-run; measured grounding=0.5, faithfulness='faithful', risk='medium'.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## The fall

The Berlin Wall fell on **9 November 1989**. The immediate trigger was a botched press briefing: an East German official announced that border crossings were open effective immediately, when the intent had been a phased process, as covered in [this history overview](https://www.history.com/topics/cold-war/berlin-wall). Crowds gathered at the checkpoints [4] and the guards, without clear orders, let them through [5].

### The night itself

The key moment was Guenter Schabowski's evening press conference on 9 November 1989. Asked when the new travel rules took effect he shuffled his notes and said, in effect, immediately. The reporting that night is summarised in [this history overview](https://www.history.com/topics/cold-war/berlin-wall), and the crowds did the rest.

sources.title: The Fall of the Berlin Wall — Britannica

initial_answers.answer_text: It fell in **1989**, on the ninth of November. The deeper causes were months of pressure: mass emigration through a newly open Hungary and the Monday demonstrations in Leipzig [1]. Further reading in [this timeline](https://timeline.example-archive.org/1989-berlin).
**9 November 1989.** The proximate cause was the confused announcement of relaxed travel rules; the structural cause was the collapse of the East German state's authority through that autumn [1].

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees: the Berlin Wall fell on 9 November 1989, triggered proximately by a garbled East German announcement that travel restrictions were lifted effective immediately, against a background of mass emigration and mounting protests.
final_synthesis.disagreement: No disagreement on the date or the trigger; the models weight the immediate press-conference cause against the deeper structural causes differently.
final_synthesis.source_support: The date and the press-conference trigger are supported by the history and encyclopaedia pages the run retrieved. Some inline markers point at source positions or external timelines the answers do not actually hold.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The exact hour the first checkpoint opened and the precise wording Schabowski used are reported slightly differently across sources.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Take 9 November 1989 and the botched-announcement trigger as well established.
